title: Using Dictate and Read Aloud in Word and Outlook
what_it_is: Dictate is a built-in Microsoft 365 tool that turns your spoken words into typed text in Word, Outlook and other Office apps. Read Aloud is a separate built-in tool that reads text on the page back to you, out loud, using a computer voice.
why_use_it: Dictate lets a learner get their ideas down without needing to type, which helps if writing by hand or typing is difficult or slow. Read Aloud lets a learner listen to a document instead of, or alongside, reading it themselves, which helps with checking work or following along with dense text.
how_to: Open Word, Outlook, or another Microsoft 365 app, and go to the Home tab at the top of the screen.
Select the Dictate button — a microphone icon, usually near the right-hand end of the Home tab.
Wait for the red recording dot to appear, then start speaking. Your words appear as typed text on the page as you talk.
Say punctuation out loud where you want it, for example 'comma' or 'full stop', and Dictate adds the symbol.
Select the Dictate button again to stop recording.
To use Read Aloud, first select the text you want read, or leave nothing selected to have it read from the top of the page.
Go to the Review tab at the top of the screen and select Read Aloud.
Use the on-screen controls to pause, skip back or forward a paragraph, or change the reading speed.
Select the X on the Read Aloud toolbar to stop and close it.
